does: This paper introduces IPI-proxy, an open-source toolkit for red-teaming web-browsing AI agents against indirect prompt injection attacks. The tool functions as an intercepting proxy that modifies HTTP responses from whitelisted domains in real time, injecting adversarial payloads into HTML pages. The system consolidates 820 deduplicated attack strings from six existing prompt-injection benchmarks into a unified library and enables systematic testing through a YAML-driven harness that parameterizes payload selection, embedding techniques (HTML comments, invisible CSS, or semantic prose), and insertion locations within pages. This allows researchers to evaluate how web-browsing agents respond to hidden instructions embedded in content from domains they are explicitly authorized to access, addressing a gap where existing red-teaming resources cannot test agents operating under strict domain whitelists.
connects: This work builds on established research in adversarial attacks against language models, specifically extending the prompt-injection threat model from direct API attacks to the indirect injection scenario prevalent in deployed web-browsing agents. It synthesizes insights and attack payloads from multiple prior benchmarking efforts (BIPIA, InjecAgent, AgentDojo, Tensor Trust, WASP, LLMail-Inject) that individually tested various injection vectors but did not systematically evaluate injection techniques or provide reusable testing infrastructure. The paper responds to limitations of generic LLM security scanners, which probe model endpoints rather than analyzing retrieved web content, and addresses the practical mismatch between existing prompt-injection benchmarks—which rely on adversary-controlled pages external to whitelists—and real-world deployment scenarios where agents interact only with approved domains that attackers may have compromised.
enables: This toolkit enables future research into the robustness of web-browsing agents against a realistic class of injection attacks that prior tools could not systematically evaluate. By providing a reusable, parameterized red-teaming framework, it facilitates comparative studies of different embedding techniques, insertion strategies, and payload variations on agent behavior. The consolidated payload library and modular design support development of stronger defenses against indirect prompt injection, such as content filtering, agent instruction isolation, and retrieval-aware prompt engineering. The approach also establishes a methodology for red-teaming deployed agents in controlled settings, potentially enabling security audits of enterprise web-browsing AI systems operating under domain restrictions and creating a foundation for adversarial robustness benchmarks tailored to realistic whitelist scenarios.
